title: Maharaja Jaswant Singh
gloss: Or the life of Jaswant Singh, the famous Raja of Marwar (Jodhpur), who fought many battles against Emperor Aurangzeb.
notes: Raj Narayan's Rajput-biography series (s11-15), all from the Dipak Rajput Press — a systematic Rajput-martial-heroism publishing program in Urdu; the Hindu-heroic-past construction relevant to homogenization/identity dynamics.

author: Ram Chandar
title: Arbindo Ghosh
gloss: An account of the life of Arbindo Ghosh, late Principal, National College, Calcutta, and better known as a Bengal Nationalist, with the extracts from his speeches.
notes: Biography of Aurobindo Ghosh with speech extracts, from the 'Indarprast National Press' — sympathetic treatment of a leading extremist/Swadeshi nationalist just after his 1909 acquittal; genuinely political content (contrast the loyalist Edward VII life at s11). Composite pagination string preserved.

title: Shaikh-ur-Rais Hakim Bu Ali Sena
gloss: An account of the life of Bu Ali Sena, the great Muhammadan philosopher and physician who flourished in the 9th century A.D.
notes: Life of Ibn Sina (Avicenna), 3rd edition — the Yunani-medicine ancestor figure (cf. the heavy Yunani medical print of Q4).

author: Sikandar Ali Khan
title: Hayat-i-Mushtaq
gloss: A short sketch of the life of Nawab Wiqar-ul-Mulk Maulvi Mushtaq Husain, Honorary Secretary, M. A.-O. College, Aligarh.
notes: Life of Nawab Waqar-ul-Mulk of the Aligarh movement — the loyalist Muslim-modernist establishment; contrast with the Aurobindo biography (s16) on the same page.

author: Ahsan
title: Chandraoli
gloss: A drama in which a fakir tries in many ways to make guilty love to a princess, Chandraoli, who is already betrothed to a prince named Ranjit Singh, but she is proof against his guiles and is ultimately married to Ranjit Singh.
notes: Parsi-theatre-style Urdu drama, 4th edition. pencil 'not available' left margin (a prior-hand availability note).

title: Bhul Bhulayan
gloss: An Urdu adaptation of Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors by Muhammad Afzal Khan.
notes: Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors as 'Bhul Bhulayan' — a Q1 Shakespeare adaptation (cf. the Hamlet/Merchant cluster of Q4); the Urdu Shakespeare vogue spans the year.

author: Jauhr, M. S.
title: Said-i-Hawas
gloss: A drama in which Dara Jan, a king, is treacherously murdered by his brother Nadir, who falsely accuses Sanjar, Dara's son, of the act, and himself takes possession of the throne. Sanjar escapes death through the cleverness of his beloved Iqbal, daughter of Nadir, and a faithful officer named Qazal. Sanjar and Qazal ultimately defeat Nadir and arrest him, but his life is spared at the intercession of Iqbal who marries Sanjar.
notes: M. S. Jauhar (the prolific dramatist/anthologist of Q4) — his stage melodrama, 3rd edition.

author: Kanhaiya Lal, Seth
title: Asir-i-Hirs
gloss: A drama in which Nasir-ud-daulah, the King of Egypt, is dethroned by his brother Changez, who is overthrown and taken prisoner by the Commander-in-Chief of the former, who restores the King to his throne.
notes: 4th edition. Bracket note: previous edition noticed at entry No. 258, on page 46 of the Catalogue for the quarter ending the 30th June 1907.

author: Tirath Ram
title: Mahadev Govind Ranade
gloss: An account of the life of the famous Mahratta reformer and judge (of the Bombay High Court) Mr. M. G. Ranade, who was born in 1842 and died in 1901. Translated from the original in English.
notes: Life of M. G. Ranade, the Maharashtra social reformer/judge — pan-Indian reform-hero biography translated from English.

title: Umar Foruq
gloss: An elaborate biography of Umar, the second Caliph of Islam, translated from an Arabic original by an Egyptian writer named Rafiq Beck.
notes: Life of Caliph Umar, 440 pp., translated from an Egyptian Arabic original — Cairo→Lahore transmission (cf. the Insha-Ullah Cairo translations of Q4); note the biographical balance across sects (Ali s3/s4 Shia-leaning, Umar here Sunni).

author: Abdul Ghafur
title: Tamashagah-i-Alam
gloss: The story of the love of Muhammad Azam, son of a millionaire of Mathra, for Kanwal Devi, the only daughter of a banker. Muhammad Azim, brother-in-law of Muhammad Azam, was passionately in love with Kanwal Devi, and when Muhammad Azam eloped with her, Muhammad Azim pursued them, shot Muhammad Azam and carried away Kanwal Devi. Muhammad Azam, however, recovered, and the matter having been placed before a Magistrate, Kanwal Devi was given to Muhammad Azam and they were married.
notes: 288-pp. romance novel, 2000 copies — a large print run for Urdu fiction. pencil X.

author: Ahmad Hussain Khan
title: Chandni aur Andhera
gloss: A novel in which a detective traces out the real murderer of a pleader who had two wives and whose first wife was charged with his murder.
notes: Urdu detective novel, 3rd edition — the emergent desi-detective genre (cf. the Philip/Chalta Purza detective at s4 same page).

author: Amjad Hussain, M.
title: Sitam Rasidah
gloss: The story of a girl named Marine who is married to a Mr. Lister, but Gulzar Singh, a friend of her husband, falls in love with her. Mr. Lister is accused of smuggling, runs away and dies abroad. Gulzar then proposes to Marine, but is himself murdered by an old man, whose daughter he had killed. Marine ultimately marries an old friend of hers, Mr. Franklin.
notes: European-set melodrama with English characters (Marine/Lister/Franklin) — the exoticized-Europe romance in Urdu.

title: Chalta Purza
gloss: The story of a smart detective named Philip who suffers great hardships, but is at last successful in discovering Miss Bermly, a very beautiful girl who had disappeared and for whose recovery hope had been abandoned.
notes: Detective 'Philip' thriller — the imported-detective (Sherlock-Holmes-adjacent) genre localized into Urdu.

author: Miraj-ud-din Ahmad
title: Mansur wa Mohna
gloss: Songs of the drama entitled Mansur and Mohna.
notes: Song-book of the drama Mansur wa Mohna, 4th edition — the Parsi-theatre song-libretto genre.

title: Chand Shah Sud Khawar
gloss: An Urdu adaptation of Shakespeare's play 'The Merchant of Venice.'
notes: Merchant of Venice adapted as prose fiction, 3rd edition — a second Q1 Merchant-of-Venice appearance (cf. the URDU—DRAMA Shakespeare cluster); Shylock reimagined as 'Chand Shah the moneylender'.

author: Charagh-ud-din
title: Qissa Qazi wa Chor
gloss: The story of a Qazi of the time of Harun-ur-Rashid who was compelled to surrender to a thief his money, horse and clothes, all of which were regained through the ingenuity of his wife. The thief was the great Caliph himself.
notes: Harun-al-Rashid trickster tale — the Arabian-Nights/Baghdad-caliphate story-world in Urdu chapbook form.

title: Do Harif Suraghrasan
gloss: Or a novel describing the enterprise of two detectives who traced thieves together with the lost property of a wealthy merchant.
notes: 'Two Rival Detectives' — the detective genre again (a whole detective-fiction cluster in Q1 Urdu fiction: s2, s4, s7, s11).

title: Ghubare men Panch Hafte
gloss: Translation of Jules Verne's 'Five Weeks in a Balloon.'
notes: Jules Verne's Five Weeks in a Balloon translated into Urdu — European scientific-adventure romance entering the vernacular market (cf. the Verne/Reynolds translations elsewhere in 1910).

author: Hargobind, Pandit
title: Dukhiya Larkiyan
gloss: An account of the sufferings of two girls of the Santal tribe of the aboriginies of India while the tribe was fighting for their liberty against the English.
notes: Santal-rebellion novel: aboriginal tribe 'fighting for their liberty against the English' — an unusually explicit anti-colonial framing (the Santal Hool of 1855-56 as sympathetic subject); a genuinely political fiction entry.

author: Kishan Singh
title: Banda Bahadur
gloss: A historical novel describing the battles fought by Banda, the renowned follower of Guru Gobind Singh, against the Moghal Emperors of Delhi.
notes: Banda Bahadur historical novel — Sikh martial-history against the Mughals; the Sikh-heroic-past construction (parallel to the Rajput series in Urdu biography), Amritsar-published.

author: Muhammad Abdullah, Mr.
title: Novel 113
gloss: A detective story which is a translation of a French original.
notes: 'Novel 113' — a French detective novel (Émile Gaboriau's Le Dossier No. 113) translated into Urdu, 450 pp.; the French roman-policier reaching Lahore.

author: Ram Lal
title: Ramuz-i-Haqiqat
gloss: A novel describing the story of a man who fell in love with a woman and was ultimately legally married to her, with a moral that pure mundane love is the stepping stone to the love of God.
publisher: Author, Teacher, Jubilee High School, Wazirabad
notes: Sufi-inflected didactic romance (earthly love as ladder to divine love) by a schoolteacher-author.
norm_publisher: Author, Teacher, Jubilee High School, Wazirabad

title: Ruqayya
gloss: A domestic novel in illustration of the pitiable condition of the Muhammadan women and their bad treatment by husbands.
notes: Reformist domestic novel on the mistreatment of Muslim women — the women's-condition/social-reform strand of Urdu fiction (cf. Nazir Ahmad's reform novels).

title: Fasana-i-Hind
gloss: A book describing some of the most important events of the ancient Indian History selected from the Ramayana and Mahabaharata translated from English.
notes: 'Ancient Indian history' distilled from the Ramayana and Mahabharata but 'translated from English' — epic-as-history mediated through an English source; the double translation (epic→English→Urdu) is exactly the observation-operator layering of interest.

title: Hindustan par Hamle
gloss: An account of the invasions on India from the North-West by the rulers of other countries in ancient and modern times, translated into Urdu by Ali Hussain from the English version of a Russian original.
notes: 'Invasions of India from the North-West' — a Russian original via English into Urdu (Russian→English→Urdu chain); the North-West-Frontier anxiety, and a rare Russian-sourced text. Note the Great-Game resonance of a Russian history of invasion routes into India.

author: Muhammad Halim
title: Tarikh-i-Usmani
gloss: A complete history of the Ottoman Empire, translated from an Arabic original by the famous Egyptian writer Colonel Ismail Beck, Inspector of Military Schools.
notes: 620-pp. history of the Ottoman Empire, 2000 copies — pan-Islamic solidarity with the Ottoman Caliphate on the eve of the Balkan crises; translated from an Egyptian Arabic source (Cairo→Lahore transmission again, cf. Umar Foruq p26). A significant pan-Islamist text.

title: Tarikh-i-Tunis wa Trablas
gloss: A short history of Tunis and Tripoli, translated by Abdul Halim from the original in Arabic by Colonel Ismail Beck, Inspector, Military School, Egypt.
notes: History of Tunis and Tripoli — North-African Muslim lands then under French/Ottoman contest (Italy would invade Tripoli in 1911); same Egyptian source-author (Ismail Beck) as s3. The pan-Islamic geographical imagination mapped through Cairo military-school historiography.

author: Sayad Mahdi, Nawab
title: Qudrati Sair Bin
gloss: A novel describing the love of a young man for his uncle's daughter. He suffers great hardships, but is at last successful in marrying her.
notes: Cousin-marriage romance novel.

author: Sharif Hussain, Sayad
title: Ijaz-i-Husn, Hissa-i-Duam
gloss: Sequel of a novel describing the guilty love of a man and a woman of noble families and the bad consequences of such connection.
notes: Part II (pp. 73-196 continuous with Part I) — moralizing romance on illicit love.

author: Zakir Husain, M., Akhtar
title: Intiqam
gloss: A historical novel describing the adventures of Salma, daughter of Hajar bin Adi, a friend of Ali, the son-in-law of the prophet Muhammad. The girl's father was murdered by Muawiya, Governor of Syria, and in order to avenge his death she went to Damascus, the capital of Syria, and got herself employed in the service of Yazid, son of Muawiya, who fell in love with her. Salma afterwards poisoned Yazid and escaped.
notes: 'Revenge' — a Shia-partisan historical revenge novel set against the Umayyads (Muawiya/Yazid as villains), a female-avenger heroine; the Karbala-adjacent narrative world in fiction form.

title: Sharah-i-Qanun-i-Miad-i-Sama'at, Hind, Act 9, 1908
gloss: Translation of Act 9 of 1908 on Limitation, annotated rendered into Urdu by Shaikh Ghulam Rasul.
notes: 862-pp. annotated Urdu translation of the Indian Limitation Act 1908 — the colonial legal code vernacularized for native practitioners; the law-in-translation genre (self-representation of the imperial statute in the vernacular market).

author: Ghulam Ahmad, M.
title: Mujarrabat-i-Qadiri
gloss: A book which contains short descriptions of important diseases and gives prescriptions for their treatment.
notes: Yunani medical formulary, 5000 copies — a very large medical print run; the Ghulam Ahmad medical publishing operation of Ludhiana (s1-3, shared copyright note).

title: Sharah-i-Qanun-i-Registry Dastawezat, Act 16, 1908
gloss: Or Act No. 16 of 1908, annotated, translated by Shaikh Ghulam Rasul.
notes: Urdu annotation of the Registration Act 1908 — Ghulam Rasul's colonial-law-translation series continues (s1-2).

author: Ghulam Ahmad, M.
title: Purana Kashmiri Kok Shastar
gloss: A book describing the different temperaments of men and women and particularly dealing with venereal diseases.
copyright: M. Ghulam Ahmad, Bookseller, Ludhiana; No. 30, dated the 28th February, 1910.
notes: Author dash-continuation of s1. 'Old Kashmiri Kok Shastra' — an erotology/sexology text (Koka Shastra tradition) reframed as venereal-disease medicine; the sexual-health quackery market. Copyright note spans s2-3.

author: Ghulam Ahmad, M.
title: Raz-i-Tib
gloss: A book which treats of diseases and gives prescriptions for their treatment.
notes: Author dash-continuation of s2 (Ghulam Ahmad). Third of his 1910Q1 medical titles.

title: Hamdard-i-Nau Jawanan
gloss: A pamphlet dealing with venereal diseases of young men addicted to bad habits and containing suggestions for their treatment.
notes: 'Friend of Youth' venereal-disease/masturbation-anxiety tract, 4000 copies at 1 anna — mass-market moral-medical panic literature; publisher 'The Sanyasi Ashram, Sargodha' (a Hindu-ascetic imprint on Muslim-associated sexual-health themes). Bracket note: previous edition at entry No. 17, page 17 of the Catalogue for the quarter ending 30th September 1909.

author: Joshi, M. S.
title: Khulasat-ul-Hikmat
gloss: A treatise containing certain instructions for the preservation of health.
notes: Hygiene/health-preservation pamphlet.

author: Karim Bakhsh
title: Ilaj-i-Daktri
gloss: A booklet describing the treatment of some important diseases according to the western system of medicine, for the use of Native Hakims and other people.
notes: 'Doctor's Treatment' — Western allopathic medicine explicitly packaged 'for the use of Native Hakims': the hybridization of Yunani and Western medicine, a direct instance of colonial knowledge being absorbed into the indigenous practitioner's repertoire (the reification/appropriation dynamic in the medical field).

author: Abdur Rahman
title: Tarbiyat
gloss: Notes and instructions for the education of children.
notes: Child-rearing/pedagogy tract — the reformist education-of-children genre.

title: Afghanistan
gloss: A brief account of the physical, geographical, historical and social conditions of Afghanistan.
notes: Country-study of Afghanistan — the frontier/Great-Game geographical curiosity (cf. the North-West-invasions history at p29 s2); Afghanistan as object of Punjabi-Muslim interest.

author: Agha Shair, Kazilbash
title: Daman-i-Maryam
gloss: A book on domestic economy dealing with cooking, sewing, embroidery, knitting and other things for girls, with essays on moral and social subjects.
notes: Domestic-economy/home-science manual for girls with moral essays — the female-domesticity curriculum (cf. Daman-i-Maryam by Agha Shair Kazilbash, a noted Urdu poet writing girls' didactic prose).

title: Mujarrabat-i-Imam Swaidi
gloss: A book containing the tried prescriptions, for various diseases, of Imam Swaidi, a well-known Muhammadan physician, rendered into Urdu by Muhammad Hussain.
notes: Prescriptions of the physician Imam Swaidi rendered into Urdu — Arabic/Yunani medical authority translated; published from Ahmadabad (a rare non-Punjab imprint place).

title: Quwwat-i-Samaat aur Kan
gloss: A booklet on the diseases of the ear, their cure and measures necessary for the preservation of hearing.
notes: Otology/hearing-preservation booklet.

author: Ram Kishan, Hakim
title: Kamil Hakim ya Vaid
gloss: A book in which the method of preparing extracts, syrups, pills, &c., both according to the Yunani and Vedic medicine systems, is explained.
notes: 'The Complete Hakim or Vaid' — explicitly synthesizes Yunani (Muslim) AND Vedic (Ayurvedic/Hindu) pharmacy in one manual; the syncretic-medicine ideal embodied in the title (hakim=Muslim, vaid=Hindu practitioner). Directly relevant to the cross-tradition homogenization theme.

author: Ram Kishan, Hakim
title: Makhzan-i-Hikmat
gloss: A pharmacopoeia, in alphabetical order, of all the medicines used by the Yunani physicians.
notes: Author dash-continuation of s9. 500-pp. alphabetical Yunani pharmacopoeia — the reference-work/systematization impulse in indigenous medicine.

title: Sihhat-ul-Abdan
gloss: Translation in Urdu by Abdus Sattar and Muhammad Nazir of an Arabic original by Dr. Abidin Khairullah of Egypt, describing some important principles and containing instructions for the preservation of general health.
notes: Egyptian hygiene manual (Dr. Abidin Khairullah of Egypt) translated into Urdu — the Cairo→Lahore knowledge channel in the medical register too.

author: Sultan Jahan Begam
title: Dai, Hissa Awwal
gloss: A booklet containing hints on midwifery.
notes: 'The Midwife', Part I — a midwifery manual authored by a woman (Sultan Jahan Begam, likely the Nawab Begam of Bhopal); a rare female-authored medical text, on women's reproductive health. Note 'authoress' in the record.

author: Ahmad Husain
title: M'alumat-i-Bosababazi
gloss: A booklet on kissing, its origin and the various customs connected with it in vogue in the different parts of the world with witty stories and couplets on kissing.
notes: A whole booklet on the customs of kissing worldwide, 3rd edition — the light/curiosity-miscellany end of the Paisa Akhbar list; a striking instance of ethnographic-cum-titillating popular print.

title: Aqaid-Nama Qwaid wa Qwanin
gloss: A booklet giving the beliefs of the Presbyterian Church in India, with the rules and regulations of the society.
notes: Presbyterian Church creed and by-laws, free distribution, from the Ludhiana Mission Press — Christian-missionary print (the low reg no. 7 signals early-year registration); the missionary presence in the Urdu print field.

author: Crosse, Revd. R. T.
title: Khandani Faiz
gloss: A pamphlet on man's duty to his wife, parents, children, other men and God, translated by the Revd. M. A. Thomas.
notes: Christian domestic-duty tract by an English missionary (Revd. R. T. Crosse), translated by an Indian pastor (Revd. M. A. Thomas) — the Christian Literature Society channel; missionary moral instruction paralleling the Muslim/Hindu conduct literature.

author: Darshna Nand
title: Editor, 'Parkash' aur Dharam Pal
gloss: A contrast of the work done by Mahashaya Krishn, Editor of the 'Parkash,' with that by Mahashaya Dharam Pal, in which the writer tries to show that the allegations against Dharam Pal are baseless.
notes: An intra-Arya-Samaj polemic (defending Dharam Pal against the editor of Prakash) — evidence of the Arya-Samaj factional press wars (the College vs Gurukul / 'Parkash' controversy); the reform-movement's internal politics playing out in cheap pamphlets.

author: Fateh-ud-din
title: Khulasa Jughrafia Zila Gujranwala
gloss: An abridged geography of the Gujranwala District.
notes: Section carries '(Designed for educational purposes.)'. Local district geography (Gujranwala) as a school text — the colonial territorial-administrative unit internalized as pedagogy; the district as the horizon of the schoolbook geography.

title: Fehrist
gloss: A list of medicines prepared by Pandit Dharam Dev sold at the Ayur Vedic Rajendr Aushadhalaya, Lahore.
notes: A free trade-catalogue/price-list of an Ayurvedic pharmacy (Rajendra Aushadhalaya) — commercial ephemera captured in the imperial bibliography; the Ayurvedic-revival dispensary as business (cf. the Arya-Samaj/Ayurveda nexus).

title: A list of the Chawla Iron Works, Gujranwala
gloss: A list of the Chawla Iron Works, Gujranwala, for 1910.
notes: A free industrial products catalogue (Chawla Iron Works) — a rare glimpse of indigenous mechanical/industrial enterprise entering the print record; the 'book' registration net catching commercial trade lists. English-titled entry.

author: Ghulam Ahmad, M.
title: Haft Ustad
gloss: A book on watch-making, photography, telegraphy, &c.
notes: 'Seven Masters' — a how-to on watch-making, photography, telegraphy — the popular-technology/self-improvement manual bringing Western mechanical arts to the vernacular reader (photography again, cf. the B. Lahora Singh photographer of Q1 p17/Q4).

author: Ghulam Qadir
title: Nur-i-Rabbani
gloss: An account of some of the miracles of Shaikh Abdul Qadir Jilani, the famous Muhammadan saint of Baghdad.
copyright: M. Muhammad Mazhar, Lahore; No. 18, dated the 14th January, 1910.
notes: Miracles of Abdul Qadir Jilani, founder of the Qadiri Sufi order — the saint-veneration/karamat genre; Baghdad sanctity in the Punjabi imagination.

author: Hakim Rai
title: Istari Siksha
gloss: A pamphlet describing the bad customs and habits prevailing among Indian women due to want of education. The advantages of female education are also described.
notes: 'Women's Education' — pro-female-education reform tract (Hindi-titled Istari Siksha from a Hindu author, in Urdu); the shared reformist consensus on women's education across communities (cf. Ruqayya p28, Daman-i-Maryam p31).

author: Hakim-ud-din
title: Kalid-i-Sanat
gloss: A book describing the construction of a fire engine with a detailed account of its machinery, the functions of the parts and instructions for repairing them, for the use of native employes of the Loco. Department of Indian Railways.
notes: 'Key to Industry' — a railway locomotive/fire-engine repair manual for native Loco. Department workers, 3rd edition; the technical-workforce literature of the colonial railway (an artisanal-industrial vernacular technical text, cf. Haft Ustad p32). Section '(Designed for educational purposes.)' applies to s15.

author: Hira Lal
title: Jughrafie-ki-Alif Be
gloss: An abridged edition of the Geographical Reader by the author.
notes: 'ABC of Geography' school reader, 9 pies (very cheap) — the mass primary-education geography primer. Marked '(Designed for educational purposes.)'.

title: Husn-i-Urdu
gloss: A pamphlet, partly in prose and partly in verse, containing essays on moral and social topics.
notes: 'The Beauty of Urdu' — a prosimetric moral-essay miscellany; the Urdu-belletristic self-cultivation of the Delhi Yusufi Press circle.

author: Ilahi Bakhsh, Nasir
title: Raz-i-Qudrat
gloss: A booklet on charms and amulets, also describing the effects of planets on human life.
copyright: M. Ilahi Bakhsh, Jullundur; No. 5, dated the 18th February, 1910.
notes: Charms, amulets and astrology (planetary effects) — the occult/talismanic strand of popular print.

author: J'afar Hussain
title: D'awat-i-Muslim-League
gloss: The writer gives an account of the two rival Muslim parties in Delhi, one headed by Haziq-ul-Mulk Hakim Muhammad Ajmal Khan and the other by Mirza Hairat, criticising the unfair means resorted to by the members of each to bring the other to grief. He also suggests that influential Muhammadans should try to bring about a reconciliation.
notes: 'Invitation to the Muslim League' — an explicitly political pamphlet on the factional split within Delhi Muslim-League politics (Hakim Ajmal Khan vs Mirza Hairat), pleading for reconciliation; free distribution. Genuinely political content documenting the organized-Muslim-politics field just after the League's 1906 founding and the 1909 Morley-Minto separate electorates.

author: Kishen Singh, B.
title: Pamphlet No. 4
gloss: A tract giving an account of the Bedi Young Men's Association, Dera Baba Nanak, Gurdaspur District, and suggesting certain social reforms among the Bedis, who are regarded as the direct descendants of Baba Nanak.
notes: Caste/biradari-reform tract by the Bedi Young Men's Association (Bedis = lineage claiming descent from Guru Nanak) — the community-association social-reform movement; the Sikh-lineage-caste self-organization.

author: Mahbub Alam, Maulvi
title: Fehrist Akhbarat-i-Hind
gloss: A book giving an alphabetical list of all the journals, magazines and newspapers, daily and weekly, published in all languages in India, with a brief account of their place of issue, annual subscription, object, etc., and a list of foreign papers.
notes: A directory of ALL Indian newspapers/periodicals in every language, by Mahbub Alam (proprietor of the Paisa Akhbar empire) — an indigenous media-census/press-directory; a self-reflexive document of the very print-public the catalogue itself surveys (a native counterpart to the imperial bibliography; second-order observation of the press field).

author: Maneshwar
title: Risala-i-Tambaku
gloss: A pamphlet on the evils of smoking.
notes: Anti-tobacco tract — the temperance/health-reform micro-genre (cf. Silk-i-Marwarid on 'book-reading and wealth' by the same author, s22).

author: Maneshwar
title: Silk-i-Marwarid
gloss: A booklet containing essays on 'Book-reading' and 'Wealth.'
notes: Author dash-continuation of s21. Essays on reading and wealth — the self-improvement moral-essay register.

author: Muhammad Abdul Ghafur
title: Mashir-i-Ma'ash
gloss: A book describing cigarette making, confectionery, poultry, painting, photography, etc., by which one can earn livelihood.
notes: 'Counsellor of Livelihood' — a how-to-earn-a-living manual (cigarette-making, poultry, photography); the vocational self-help/petty-entrepreneurship genre (cf. Haft Ustad p32). Ironic pairing with the anti-tobacco tract (s21) — cigarette-making as a trade to learn.

printer_verbatim: The Civil and Military Organ Press, Ludhiana.
author: Muhammad Hussain Khan
title: Falsfa-i-Jang
gloss: The philosophy of war. A booklet containing instructions to Native Officers of the British Indian Army regarding the principles of warfare.
notes: 'Philosophy of War' — military-science instruction for Native Officers of the British Indian Army; the martial-professionalization literature (Punjab as the 'martial races' recruiting ground). Dual pricing superior/inferior edition.

author: Muhammad Muqtada Khan
title: Almaqbul
gloss: A collection of lectures on social, moral, religious and political subjects by the author.
notes: A lecture-collection spanning 'social, moral, religious and political subjects' — the public-lecture/reform-oratory genre in print. 'Pice' misprint for Price preserved in reading.

author: Muhammad Naim
title: Faryad-i-Bezaban
gloss: A pamphlet directed against meat eating.
publisher: Author, First Teacher, English School, Nagod State, Bundehlkhand
notes: 'The Plea of the Voiceless' — an anti-meat-eating/vegetarianism tract by a Muslim schoolteacher, printed at the Arya (Hindu-reform) Steam Press; the ahimsa/vegetarian-reform current crossing communal lines (a Muslim author on a characteristically Hindu-reform theme, via an Arya press). Author based in a Central-Indian princely state (Nagod, Bundelkhand) — an out-of-province registrant.
norm_publisher: Author, First Teacher, English School, Nagod State, Bundehlkhand

author: Nabi Bakhsh, M.
title: Jantri, San 1910
gloss: Calendar for the year 1910.
notes: An almanac/calendar (jantri) for 1910, 2000 copies free — the calendrical/almanac genre; note it registers in the very quarter it opens (March 1910).

title: Nairang-i-Fasahat
gloss: A collection of the discourses, sermons and speeches delivered by Ali, the fourth Caliph of the prophet Muhammad, and the letters written by him, translated into Urdu by Zakir Hussain from the original Arabic Nahjul Balaghat.
notes: The Nahj al-Balagha (sermons/letters of Ali) translated into Urdu, 560 pp. — the foundational Shia text of Ali's eloquence; a major Shia devotional-literary undertaking (cf. the two lives of Ali at p24). The Ali-centred print of this quarter is substantial.

author: Nand Lal
title: Gae
gloss: The picture of the cow, with a short account of its form, habits etc.
publisher: Nand Lal, B.A., and Co., Co-operative Works

notes: Another Shia imamate polemic denouncing the first three caliphs as usurpers — a whole cluster of Shia controversial works (s16-19) from the Yusufi/Newal Kishore presses; the Shia-Sunni succession dispute saturates this section. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

notes: Author dash-continuation of s16 (Sajjad Hussain). Defends Ali's direct succession — the famous Newal Kishore Press (Lucknow's great publisher, here with a Lahore imprint) in the Shia-polemic trade. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Aftab-i-Khilafat
gloss: A controversial book in which the writer tries to prove that Ali was the only rightful successor of the prophet Muhammad.

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Sharh-i-Kauz-i-Maktum
gloss: A controversial book criticising the Sunni belief that Ali, the fourth Caliph, of his own free will, married his daughter, named Umm-i-Kalsum, to Umar, the second Caliph. The author has presented the case in a legal form in which the Sunnis are complainants and the Shias defendants; issues are framed; and after listening to the evidence produced by the parties and their counsels, judgment is delivered on the merits of the case, which is in favour of the Shias.
notes: A remarkable Shia polemic cast as a MOCK COURT TRIAL (Sunnis as plaintiffs, Shias as defendants, framed issues, evidence, verdict for the Shias) over the Umm Kulthum-Umar marriage — the colonial legal-procedural form appropriated as a rhetorical device for sectarian argument; a vivid instance of the courtroom paradigm colonizing religious disputation (directly on-theme: the imperial juridical form reifying/reshaping indigenous debate). Author Sajjad Hussain's fourth Shia work this quarter. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sanaullah, Maulvi

Yet another distinct sectarian front (Ahl-i-Hadith reformism) in this dense polemical section.

Directly on-theme for the script/language disaggregation the project studies. Section is 'URDU (PUNJABI CHARACTER)'.

Brown, M.D.), from the Ludhiana Mission Press; the missionary-medical-women's-health nexus AND the Roman-Urdu experiment (the colonial/missionary push to romanize Urdu). Both a female-authored medical text (cf. p31 s12) and a script-politics artefact. Composite pagination 8,116,4.

author: Muhammad Bashir

gloss: A controversial book in which the writer tries to prove that no prayer can be acceptable until Ummul Kitab, or the first verse of the Kuran, is repeated.

Composite pagination 196,8.

The quarter's Shia devotional program is comprehensive (hagiography + theology + polemic + this liturgy).

The takhmis form itself is a mode of vernacular appropriation of the Persian master-text.

By a Hindu author (Chiranji Lal). On-theme for both moral-reform and the music/performance-culture displacement.

author: Muhammad Abdullah
title: Faiz-ul-Islam
gloss: A controversial book written in refutation of another entitled Zinat-ul-Islam. In Faiz-ul-Islam the author tries to prove that according to Islam it is permissible to seek the intercession of saints and for this purpose gives quotations from the Koran and traditions.
copyright: M. Faiz-ul-Hasan, Ferozepore; No. 16, dated the 28th February 1910.
notes: A 623-pp. Barelvi-style defence of saintly intercession (tawassul) refuting an opposing (Deobandi/Wahhabi) tract — the Barelvi vs Deobandi controversy over saint-veneration, in Punjabi (Shahmukhi)+Urdu; the intra-Sunni battle over popular Sufi practice. A massive polemical tome. Complements the anti-intercession Hindu tract at p41 s7 — the intercession question fought on two fronts.

title: Sandhia mai Vidhi
gloss: Or the daily Hindu prayer, with the method of saying it.
notes: The Sandhya (daily Hindu prayer ritual) with method — Sanskrit prayer with Urdu instruction; the everyday-Hindu-liturgy counterpart to the Muslim Namaz prayer-books (cf. p44 Namaz). Sanskrit sacred text mediated through Urdu-script instruction for practical use.

title: Majmua-i-Sih Kitabi
gloss: A collection of three treatises, namely, Amad Nama, Dawaya and Sihwaya, giving inflections in grammar, the first two in Persian and Sindhi and the third in Arabic, Persian and Sindhi.
notes: A TRILINGUAL grammar-primer (Arabic/Persian/Sindhi verb-conjugation treatises), 3rd ed. — the maktab language-drill tradition; a single artefact carrying three languages, the layered Persianate literacy of the Sindhi Muslim schoolboy. Via Pokhar Das (Shikarpur) again. Vivid instance of multilingual pedagogical stacking.

title: Shri Mad Bhagwad Gita
gloss: The text of the Gita, with Hindi and Urdu translations by Diwan Maya Das and English translation by Mrs. Annie Besant.
notes: A remarkable POLYGLOT Bhagavad Gita: Sanskrit text + Hindi + Urdu (by Diwan Maya Das) + ENGLISH translation by MRS. ANNIE BESANT — the Theosophist leader's Gita rendering bound into a four-language Punjabi edition; a striking node where Theosophy, the Home-Rule-era English-educated readership, and the vernacular scripture-market converge. The single most multivalent observation-operator artefact of the quarter (one sacred text refracted through four languages and a European mediator). Composite pagination 18,604,149. Flag for the theory/bridge analysis.
